figures.gloss: The Augustinian friar and Wittenberg professor whose Ninety-Five Theses (1517) against the sale of indulgences detonated the Reformation. His doctrine of justification by faith alone — the sinner declared righteous by trust in Christ, not by merit — and his German Bible put the sacred goods directly into lay hands.
figures.facet: A reformer, not a prophet: he claimed no new revelation, only that the church had buried the old one — the rare founder whose whole authority rests on pointing away from himself to a text.
figures.provenance: Ninety-Five Theses (1517); Heidelberg Disputation (1518); Luther's German New Testament (1522); Roland Bainton, Here I Stand (1950).

figures.gloss: The French exile whose Institutes of the Christian Religion (1536; definitive 1559) systematized the Reformation and whose Geneva became its training city. His signature is the absolute sovereignty of God — providence and predestination ordering every event, election resting on God's decree alone.
figures.facet: Where Luther supplies the Reformation's fire, Calvin supplies its architecture: a total system in which divine sovereignty functions almost as impersonal cosmic law, and disciplined worldly labor becomes the arena of obedience.
figures.provenance: Institutes of the Christian Religion (1536/1559); Geneva's Ecclesiastical Ordinances (1541); Bruce Gordon, Calvin (2009).

figures.gloss: The formal principle of the Reformation: Scripture alone is the final authority in the church, standing above pope, council, and tradition. Staked publicly when Luther refused to recant at Worms (1521) unless convinced 'by Scripture and plain reason.'
figures.facet: It relocates the sacred center from an institution to a book — and in Protestant theology the preached Word does not merely inform but effects: faith itself is created by hearing it, a genuinely performative doctrine of language.
figures.provenance: Luther at the Diet of Worms (1521); Formula of Concord (1577); Westminster Confession of Faith (1646).

figures.gloss: The doctrine, argued from 1 Peter 2:9, that every baptized Christian stands before God without a mediating priestly caste — free to read Scripture, pray, and intercede for neighbors. Clergy differ by office and training, not by ontological grade.
figures.facet: The Reformation's most corrosive social idea: it dissolves the sacred/profane division of labor, and its long downstream runs through congregationalism and revivalism to every movement that hands religious authority to the unlicensed individual.
figures.provenance: 1 Peter 2:9; Luther, To the Christian Nobility of the German Nation (1520).

figures.gloss: The datable personal crisis of conversion — conviction of sin, surrender, assurance — that the evangelical wave made the gate of authentic Christianity. Modeled on John 3 ('born again'), normalized by the Great Awakenings and Wesley's Aldersgate experience (1738).
figures.facet: It moves initiation from an infant sacrament administered by the church to an adult ordeal undergone by the self — the believer must be able to narrate the moment the old person died, which makes testimony itself a liturgical form.
figures.provenance: John 3:3; Jonathan Edwards, A Faithful Narrative of the Surprising Work of God (1737); John Wesley's Journal, 24 May 1738; David Bebbington, Evangelicalism in Modern Britain (1989).

figures.gloss: The Anglican priest and itinerant field-preacher (1703-1791) who built the Methodist movement — field preaching, the society, the class meeting, the circuit rider — and taught a further 'second work of grace,' entire sanctification, atop the new birth. His own evangelical conversion, the heart 'strangely warmed' while a Moravian preface to Romans was read aloud at a society meeting on Aldersgate Street (24 May 1738), became the template testimony of the whole revivalist tradition downstream of him.
figures.facet: He turns the New Birth from doctrine into infrastructure: the class meeting he invented converts the crisis-conversion into a repeatable, examined, weekly technology — Methodism institutionalizes the datable heart-experience the corpus's own protestantism-new-birth entry already leans on.
figures.provenance: Wesley's Journal (24 May 1738 entry); Wesley, 'Christian Perfection' (1741 sermon); Richard Heitzenrater, Wesley and the People Called Methodists (1995).

figures.gloss: The radical wing of the Reformation, beginning with the first adult rebaptisms at Zurich in January 1525 (Conrad Grebel, Felix Manz): believer's baptism upon confession of faith, strict separation of church from state, and refusal of the oath and the sword. Catholic and magisterial-Protestant authorities alike executed Anabaptists by drowning, burning, and beheading through the 16th century; the Münster Rebellion (1534-35), where radicals under Jan van Leiden proclaimed an apocalyptic polygamous kingdom before its bloody suppression, became the movement's byword for catastrophe even as its pacifist Mennonite and Hutterite descendants renounced violence entirely.
figures.facet: One movement holds both halves of a martyr/millenarian mirror at once: the same baptismal radicalism produced the quiet suffering church of the Martyrs' Mirror and the armed apocalyptic commune at Münster — identical premises (a gathered, purified church awaiting the end) resolving into pacifist withdrawal or violent theocracy depending on circumstance.
figures.provenance: The Schleitheim Confession (1527); Thieleman van Braght, Martyrs' Mirror (1660); George H. Williams, The Radical Reformation (1962); standard accounts of Zurich 1525 and Münster 1534-35.

figures.gloss: The 1611 'Authorized Version,' commissioned by James I from a committee of scholars working from the Bishops' Bible, Tyndale, and the original languages — the translation that became the de facto Bible of the English-speaking Protestant world for over three centuries, from the Puritans who carried it to New England to the missionary Bibles that shaped Anglophone Christianity on every continent.
figures.facet: A translation whose authority arrived by cadence rather than argument: unlike sola scriptura's case for Scripture's supremacy in principle, the KJV became authoritative through the sound of its prose — the version whose rhythms are still quoted by people who could not name the doctrine of sola scriptura at all.
figures.provenance: The 1611 preface, 'The Translators to the Reader'; David Daniell, The Bible in English (2003); Adam Nicolson, God's Secretaries (2003).

figures.gloss: The Northampton, Massachusetts pastor-philosopher (1703-1758) whose A Faithful Narrative of the Surprising Work of God (1737) turned the local revival under his preaching into the first sustained theological analysis of mass conversion, and whose Religious Affections (1746) tried to distinguish genuine grace from revivalist excess after the First Great Awakening's controversies. His sermon 'Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God' (1741) is the era's most quoted specimen of the terror that could precede the new birth.
figures.facet: He is the Awakening's theologian-observer rather than its popular evangelist: where Wesley organizes the convert, Edwards clinically distinguishes counterfeit conviction from the real thing, treating the datable conversion crisis as a phenomenon to be tested against marks, not simply proclaimed.
figures.provenance: Jonathan Edwards, A Faithful Narrative of the Surprising Work of God (1737); Religious Affections (1746); George Marsden, Jonathan Edwards: A Life (2003).

figures.gloss: The Zurich reformer and city preacher (1484-1531) who led the Swiss Reformation independently of Luther, reduced the mass to a quarterly memorial meal, and died on the battlefield at Kappel defending Zurich's Protestant cantons. At the Marburg Colloquy (1529) he and Luther agreed on fourteen of fifteen articles but split irreconcilably over the Eucharist — Luther insisting Christ's body is truly present 'in, with, and under' the bread, Zwingli reading 'this is my body' as 'this signifies my body.'
figures.facet: He anchors the low end of a sacramental spectrum the corpus's Luther and Calvin entries only imply: if the Supper is sign and remembrance rather than vehicle of presence, Protestantism's sacramental register runs all the way down to memorialism — Marburg is the moment the Reformation discovers it cannot agree with itself on what the sacrament is.
figures.provenance: Marburg Colloquy records (1529); Zwingli, On the Lord's Supper (1526); G. R. Potter, Zwingli (1976).

figures.gloss: The English Reformed Protestants, active from the Elizabethan settlement through the Restoration, who judged the Church of England insufficiently purged of Catholic ceremony and sought a fully reformed church on Genevan lines; their emigrant congregations founded Plymouth (1620) and Massachusetts Bay (1630), covenanting both church and civil community under a self-understanding as a New Israel — John Winthrop's 'city upon a hill' — and prizing introspective self-examination of one's own election.
figures.facet: New England Puritanism supplies the psychological infrastructure — the anxious, diary-keeping, sign-reading self scrutinizing its own soul for evidence of election — that the corpus's later American movements (Shakers, Adventism, Christian Science, Mormonism) inherit and redirect even where they reject Calvinist theology outright.
figures.provenance: John Winthrop, 'A Model of Christian Charity' (1630); the Cambridge Platform (1648); Perry Miller, The New England Mind (1939); David Hall, Worlds of Wonder, Days of Judgment (1989).

figures.gloss: Congregational vernacular hymn-singing: Luther's own chorales ('Ein feste Burg ist unser Gott'), the metrical psalters of the English and Scottish churches, Isaac Watts's hymns (from 1707), and Charles Wesley's roughly 6,500 hymn texts, which set Methodist doctrine to music sung from memory by people who never read a page of systematic theology.
figures.facet: It is Protestantism's answer to the sacramental image it removed: where the Mass gave the illiterate laity theology through the eye — stained glass, icon, elevated host — the hymn gives it through the ear and the body's own voice, doctrine internalized by repetition and melody rather than beheld.
figures.provenance: Luther's chorale 'Ein feste Burg' (c.1529); Isaac Watts, Hymns and Spiritual Songs (1707); Charles Wesley's hymn corpus; J. R. Watson, The English Hymn (1997).

edges.note: Sola scriptura is Luther's formal principle — Scripture over pope and council, staked at Leipzig (1519) and Worms (1521).
Luther articulated the universal priesthood in To the Christian Nobility (1520) as the demolition charge under the clerical estate.

GOLD SEAM: Direct descent — the Reformation is a reform within Latin Christianity, and Luther's theology of the cross re-centers the whole tradition on the Pauline atonement, claiming recovery, not new revelation.
GOLD SEAM: Same Christ, same creeds — Protestantism keeps Nicaea and Chalcedon intact and disputes only the mediating apparatus between Christ and the believer.

GOLD SEAM (downstream): New Thought grew from New England's Protestant milieu — Quimby, post-Unitarian Transcendentalism, Emma Curtis Hopkins — radicalizing the unmediated believer into a mind that directly works the Divine.

GOLD SEAM: The Protestant conviction that the Word itself effects what it declares is secularized in New Thought's affirmation — the sermon's performative speech re-tooled as self-administered decree.
The structural inverse of graded initiatory secrecy: the Reformation prints the innermost text in the vernacular and hands it to every plowboy.
GOLD SEAM: Direct descent — Pentecostalism was born inside the Wesleyan-Holiness wing of Protestantism, and Spirit baptism is framed as a further work of grace presupposing the evangelical new birth.
GOLD SEAM: Azusa radicalized the universal priesthood — the Spirit falling on women, laborers, and the formally unordained, who preached and prophesied on the spot.

GOLD SEAM: Direct descent — Miller was a licensed Baptist preacher and Millerism arose wholly inside revivalist Protestantism; his prophetic arithmetic is sola scriptura radicalized into a layman's equation.
GOLD SEAM: Eddy's Congregationalist inheritance radicalized — the Protestant relocation of authority from clergy to text is completed by ordaining two books as the church's only pastor.

GOLD SEAM: direct descent — Friends emerged from the radical Puritan and Seeker wing of the English Reformation, driving the universal priesthood to its terminus: no clergy, no outward sacraments, the Light immediate in every person.
Within the same family, the formal principle inverted: Friends set the living Spirit above the letter — Scripture is confirmed by the Light that gave it, and worship needs no book at all.

GOLD SEAM: Scripture-alone raised against an authorized interpretive tradition — the 'Protestants of Judaism' label is a later comparative analogy; Karaism precedes the Reformation by seven centuries and no contact is claimed.
Aldersgate (1738) is the new-birth entry's own paradigm case, already cited in that entry's provenance.

Wesley's doctrine of a second work of grace (entire sanctification) is the direct ancestor of the Wesleyan-Holiness movement, out of which Pentecostal Spirit-baptism at Azusa Street emerged as a further work of grace.
Anabaptists radicalized the universal priesthood into a visible church of professing adults only — believer's baptism as the entrance gate a state-church infant baptism could never be.

Both reject an intermediary clergy and a state church as part of the Reformation's radical/spiritualist wing, though Quakers arise in England over a century later with no direct Anabaptist contact claimed.
The KJV is the material vehicle sola scriptura needed: an English Bible authoritative enough to put the sole rule of faith directly into lay hands.
Edwards's Faithful Narrative is cited directly in the new-birth entry's own provenance as the theological analysis of the datable conversion crisis.
Edwards preached from Solomon Stoddard's Northampton pulpit and inherited New England's Puritan covenant theology and introspective piety directly.
Marburg (1529): real sacramental presence versus memorial sign — the two poles of the Reformation's Eucharistic dispute, never reconciled.
Puritanism is a scripture-focused reform movement within the Church of England seeking to complete what the Reformation's formal principle demanded.
New England's 19th-c. revivalist descendants, including Millerism, grow from soil the Puritan covenant churches first tilled two centuries earlier; no direct doctrinal descent claimed.
Luther's own chorales, from the 1520s, are hymnody's founding exhibit.
Congregational hymn-singing enacts the universal priesthood in practice: the whole gathered laity, not a trained choir or clergy, is the liturgical voice.
Calvin's Geneva tried and burned Servetus for heresy in 1553 — the tradition's founding martyrdom, delivered by magisterial Protestantism's own hand.

dossier.essence: Protestantism is the family of Christian churches descending from the sixteenth-century Reformation — Lutheran, Reformed and Presbyterian, Anglican, and the later Baptist, Methodist, and evangelical bodies — today global and numbering roughly 800 million to a billion depending on whether independent churches are counted. Its central claim is that sinners are justified by grace through faith in Christ alone, and that Scripture — not pope, council, or tradition — is the church's final authority.
dossier.cosmos: It keeps classical Christian cosmology intact: one triune God creating from nothing, the Fall, redemption through the incarnate Christ's cross and resurrection, linear history ending in judgment and a renewed creation. What the Reformation redrew was the map of salvation rather than of the cosmos — purgatory and the treasury of merit are rejected, so at death the soul passes to Christ's presence or to separation from him, awaiting bodily resurrection, with nothing the living can purchase to alter the outcome.
dossier.practice: Worship centers on the preached Word, with the sermon and vernacular Bible at the heart of the assembly and, in most bodies, two sacraments — baptism and the Lord's Supper — in place of the medieval seven. Its ethical signature is vocation: ordinary labor, marriage, and citizenship as arenas of divine calling rather than a lower track beneath monasticism. Institutionally it has no single center — state churches, presbyterian assemblies, and congregational independency coexist, and schism is the family's structural habit as much as reform is its founding gesture.
dossier.canon: The Protestant Bible — the Hebrew-canon Old Testament without the deuterocanonical books, plus the twenty-seven-book New Testament — as sole final authority; confessional documents such as the Augsburg Confession (1530), the Westminster Confession (1646), and the Book of Common Prayer hold subordinate, Scripture-normed status.
dossier.reflects: The reforming impulse itself — the recurring conviction that the institution has buried the original revelation, and that religious authority belongs finally to the text and the unmediated conscience of the believer.
sources: Martin Luther, 'Disputation on the Power and Efficacy of Indulgences (The Ninety-Five Theses)' (1517)
Mark A. Noll, 'Protestantism: A Very Short Introduction' (2011)
Roland H. Bainton, 'Here I Stand: A Life of Martin Luther' (1950)

links.cite: Martin Luther, 'Disputation on the Power and Efficacy of Indulgences (The Ninety-Five Theses)' (1517)
Mark A. Noll, 'Protestantism: A Very Short Introduction' (2011)
Roland H. Bainton, 'Here I Stand: A Life of Martin Luther' (1950)
